name: Spring-Style E-Commerce Framework — Generic Interface Capstone
scenario: Build a mini Spring Data-style framework from scratch. Using only generic interfaces and implementations — no frameworks, no annotations — create the same patterns that Spring Data, MapStruct, and validation frameworks use internally. This demonstrates you truly understand how production Java frameworks are built.
description: A complete mini framework using generic interfaces: Repository pattern for data access, Mapper for type conversion, Validator for validation pipelines, Builder for complex object construction. Uses all Module 9 concepts: generic classes, generic methods, bounded type parameters, wildcards in API design, and generic interfaces.

core.description:
Framework interfaces:
- `Repository<T, ID>` — CRUD with default exists(), count(), saveAll()
- `Mapper<S, T>` — mapTo, mapFrom, default mapAllTo
- `Validator<T>` — isValid(T), default and(), or(), negate()
- `Builder<T>` — build()

All with appropriate generic type parameters.
`InMemoryRepository<T, ID>` — generic class implementing Repository<T, ID> using HashMap. Works for any entity type.
Domain: Product, Order, Customer entities. For each: a concrete Repository, a DTO class, a Mapper<Entity, DTO>, and at least one Validator<Entity>.

`OrderService` that composes Repository<Order,String>, Repository<Customer,Integer>, Mapper<Order,OrderDto>, and a chain of Validator<Order> instances. Methods: placeOrder(OrderDto), getOrderHistory(int customerId) → List<OrderDto>.
`OrderBuilder implements Builder<Order>` — fluent construction with at least 5 fields.
main() demonstrates the full flow: build order (Builder) → validate (Validator composition) → save (Repository) → fetch → convert to DTO (Mapper).
Zero raw types. Correct generic bounds where needed. No unchecked casts.

bonus.description: Add `QueryRepository<T, ID>` extending Repository<T, ID> with `List<T> findWhere(Predicate<T> condition)` — uses wildcards in the Predicate type.
Add `Pipeline<T>` functional interface (composable with then()) and use it to create a processing pipeline for orders: validate → enrich → save.
Add a `Statistics<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>` reuse from Topic 9.3 — compute order amount statistics.

data_to_use:
Domain data for demonstration:
- Customers: Aisha(1), Raj(2), Priya(3)
- Products: Mouse(P01, ₹799), Keyboard(P02, ₹1999), USB Hub(P03, ₹499)
- Orders to place:
  * ORD-001: Customer Aisha, Product Mouse, qty=2, amount=1598
  * ORD-002: Customer Raj, Product Keyboard, qty=1, amount=1999
  * ORD-003: Invalid order (amount=-500) — should be rejected
example_output:
=== E-Commerce Framework Demo ===

[Builder] Built: ORD-001 | Mouse x2 | ₹1598.0
[Builder] Built: ORD-002 | Keyboard x1 | ₹1999.0
[Builder] Built: ORD-003 | Monitor x1 | ₹-500.0

[Validator] ORD-001: VALID
[Validator] ORD-002: VALID
[Validator] ORD-003: INVALID — Amount must be positive

[Repository] Saved 2 orders

[Mapper] ORD-001 DTO: {orderId=ORD-001, customer=Aisha, item=Mouse x2, total=₹1598.0}
[Mapper] ORD-002 DTO: {orderId=ORD-002, customer=Raj, item=Keyboard x1, total=₹1999.0}

[Repository] Aisha's orders: 1
[Repository] Total orders: 2
ai_policy_explanation: This is the Module 9 capstone — all five generics topics synthesized. No AI assistance. If stuck: re-read the Repository, Mapper, Validator sections from Topic 9.5, and the generic class/method sections from 9.1-9.2. Every concept used here appeared in a previous topic.
submission_instructions: Save as EcommerceFramework.java. Add Javadoc to each interface explaining the type parameters. Push to GitHub with commit message: 'Module 9 capstone: Generic interface mini-framework'. This is portfolio-grade code.
tips_for_success: Start with the four interfaces — get them compiling before any implementation.
InMemoryRepository<T, ID>: uses HashMap<ID, T>. findAll returns ArrayList(store.values()). save puts into map.
Validator.and() default: `return item -> isValid(item) && other.isValid(item)` — lambda returning new Validator.
OrderService constructor takes Repository and Mapper — constructor injection, same as Spring.
For placeOrder: take OrderDto, mapFrom to Order, run validators, save if valid.
getOrderHistory: findAll (or filtered if you implement bonus QueryRepository), map to DTOs.
concepts_reinforced: All five Module 9 generics topics synthesized
